1: Say to your brothers, ‘My people,’ and to your sisters, ‘My beloved’ — the reversal of Lo-ammi and Lo-ruhamah is stated immediately after the promise of restoration (Hosea 1:10-11). The Hebrew is ammi and ruhamah — the names of Hosea’s children with the negative prefix removed. This verse functions as a bridge between the oracle of judgment (Hosea 1) and the extended covenant-lawsuit that follows in chapter 2: even as YHWH prosecutes Israel, the restoration is already in view.
2: Contend with your mother, contend (rivu be’imkem rivu) — the repetition signals the formal opening of a rib (covenant lawsuit, legal dispute). The rib pattern is a distinctive prophetic genre in which YHWH calls Israel to account as in a court of law, often with creation or the nations as witnesses (Micah 6:1-2; Isaiah 1:2-3). She is not my wife, and I am not her husband — the formal declaration of divorce using the standard ANE divorce formula (attested in Akkadian documents: “She is not my wife; I am not her husband”). YHWH is using the divorce proceedings of his own culture to describe the covenant dissolution, while simultaneously pleading for Gomer/Israel to remove her unfaithfulness — the divorce is not yet final.
3: I will strip her naked and expose her as on the day she was born — in ANE legal practice, public exposure of a woman guilty of adultery or prostitution was part of the divorce and shaming process. The reversal “as on the day she was born” strips away all the covenant gifts YHWH gave Israel in the exodus: the clothing (metaphorical, the covenant privileges), the land, the fruitfulness. Make her like a wilderness, like a parched land — the reversal of Canaan’s agricultural fertility back to the pre-conquest wilderness; the land’s gifts are revealed as YHWH’s, not the Baals’.
4: I will show no compassion to her children, for they are children of unfaithfulness — the children (Israel) share the identity of their mother’s unfaithfulness. This reflects the ANE understanding of family solidarity: the household’s guilt is shared. The theological point is not vindictive but diagnostic: a society shaped by its parents’ idolatry inherits both the practices and the consequences. The distinction from Hosea 2:3 (the mother = the nation’s religious leadership or history?) and Hosea 2:4 (the children = the current generation?) may reflect the prophetic move from structural criticism to personal accountability.
5: She said, ‘I will go after my lovers, who give me my bread and my water, my wool and my flax, my oil and my wine’ — the lovers (meʾahavim) are the Baals, the Canaanite storm and fertility deities. The theological core of Baal worship was the belief that Baal controlled the rain, and therefore the grain harvest. The list — bread, water, wool, flax, oil, wine — is the standard inventory of agricultural prosperity in ancient Israel. The error is crediting the gifts to the wrong giver. Archaeological evidence from the northern kingdom shows widespread Baal worship alongside YHWH worship: Baal figurines, Asherah poles, and high-place installations have been excavated at numerous northern sites.
6: I will block her way with thorns; I will wall her in — YHWH’s response to Israel’s pursuit of the Baals is to make that pursuit impossible through historical circumstances. The “thorns” and “wall” are divine obstacles — historically, the Assyrian invasions and political instability that disrupted the northern kingdom’s prosperity and cult practices. The verb sakh (to hedge in) appears in Job 3:23 (God hedges in Job so he cannot move) and, positively, in Job 1:10 (God hedges Job in for protection) — the same divine action can be shelter or prison depending on one’s orientation to YHWH.
7: She will say, ‘I will go back to my first husband, for it was better for me then than now’ — the wilderness period is idealized as the “first marriage” between YHWH and Israel. In the wilderness, Israel depended entirely on YHWH for manna, water, and protection; in Canaan, that dependence was transferred to the Baals. The “first husband” framing positions the Sinai covenant as the original marriage, and the Baal worship as adultery against it. Jeremiah 2:2 makes the same idealizing move: “I remember the devotion of your youth, your love as a bride, how you followed me in the wilderness.”
8: She did not know that it was I who gave her the grain, the wine, and the oil — did not know (lo yadaʿah) is the central diagnostic: the “knowledge of God” (daʿat ʾelohim) that Hosea identifies as the missing element in Israel’s relationship is precisely the recognition that YHWH is the source of all agricultural blessing. The Baal cult claimed that Baal’s rain produced the harvest; YHWH’s counter-claim is that he is the actual provider. Silver and gold which they used for Baal — Israel used YHWH’s material blessings (silver and gold) to fund the cult of the Baals, the very deity credited with the blessings. The theological irony is complete.
9: I will take back my grain when it ripens and my wine when it is ready — the grammar emphasizes YHWH’s ownership: “my grain,” “my wine” — everything Israel attributed to Baal actually belongs to YHWH, and he can reclaim it. This is the agricultural form of covenant curse (Deuteronomy 28:30-33, 38-42): the harvest you plant will be taken from you. Uncover her nakedness before her lovers — the removal of agricultural prosperity is simultaneously the public exposure of Israel’s shame; the fertility cult’s failure to deliver fertility is itself the proof of Baal’s impotence.
10: Now I will expose her shameful nakedness before her lovers, and no one will rescue her — the ANE context of this verse is the legal public shaming of an adulteress: her lovers witness her punishment and prove powerless to help. In Israel’s historical experience this is the Assyrian invasion: Baal could not prevent the agricultural and military catastrophe, even though Israel had burned incense to him at every high place. The claim of the fertility cult — that Baal controlled weather and military victory — is disproved precisely when Israel needed those things most.
11: I will put an end to all her celebrations — her festivals, new moon observances, sabbaths, and all her appointed feasts — YHWH cancels Israel’s liturgical calendar. The moʿadim (appointed feasts) were the three pilgrimage festivals (Passover, Weeks, Tabernacles); the chodashim (new moons) were monthly celebrations; the shabbatot (sabbaths) were the weekly marker of covenant identity. All of these had been conflated with or conducted alongside Baal worship. Their cessation means the complete collapse of the religious year. The Assyrian destruction of the northern sanctuaries (Bethel, Dan) in 722 BCE was the historical fulfillment.
12: I will ruin her vines and her fig trees, which she said were the gifts her lovers gave her — vines and figs are the emblems of shalom in the land (1 Kings 4:25: “Judah and Israel lived in safety, everyone under his own vine and fig tree”). The destruction of the vineyards and orchards — made into “a thicket where wild animals devour” — reverses the covenant land’s productivity back to a pre-agricultural wilderness. This agricultural destruction was literal: Assyrian campaigns routinely cut down orchards and destroyed irrigation systems as a strategy of economic devastation (attested in Assyrian royal annals).
13: For the days she burned incense to the Baals, adorning herself with rings and jewels and going after her lovers — the “days of the Baals” describes the cycle of Baal festivals, which involved elaborate preparation: the female worshiper (here Israel, metaphorically) puts on jewelry and fine clothing as she goes to the shrine. The rings and jewels may indicate either the adornment of cult images or the worshipers themselves at the high-place festivals. The phrase vatishkacheni neʾum YHWH — “she forgot me, declares the LORD” — is the theological core: the sin is not ignorance but willful forgetting, the active suppression of the covenant memory.
14: Therefore, behold, I will allure her and bring her into the wilderness and speak tenderly to her — the divine response to Israel’s unfaithfulness is not only judgment but a second courtship. The wilderness (midbar) is the place of YHWH’s original relationship with Israel (the Sinai period), before Canaan’s prosperity led to Baal worship. “Speak tenderly” (dibarti ʿal-libah, “speak to her heart”) is the language of courtship (Genesis 34:3; Ruth 2:13; Judges 19:3). The exile — which stripped Israel of land, temple, and king — is reinterpreted as a divinely-arranged wilderness for a new covenant relationship.
15: There I will give back her vineyards and make the Valley of Achor a door of hope — the Valley of Achor (“Valley of Trouble”) was where Achan was executed for covenant violation after the conquest (Joshua 7:24-26); it became proverbial for the consequence of betrayal. YHWH promises to transform the very symbol of covenant judgment into a door of hope: the entry-point into the land (the Jordan valley area) that was the scene of covenant failure becomes the entry-point of restoration. The vineyard restoration reverses Hosea 2:12. She will respond there as in the days of her youth, as in the day she came out of Egypt — the second exodus theme: Israel will respond to YHWH’s renewed courtship as she responded at Sinai.
16: You will call me ‘My Husband’ (ishi) and will no longer call me ‘My Master’ (baali) — this verse is built on a Hebrew wordplay. Ishi means “my husband/my man” (the intimate partner); baali means “my lord/master” and is also the name of the Canaanite god Baal. Israel calling YHWH “my Baal” as a title of lordship had become indistinguishable from invoking the name of the rival deity. The eschatological restoration removes this ambiguity: the covenant relationship will be so intimate that Israel will address YHWH with the most personal term for a husband, not a term confusable with Baal’s name.
17: I will remove the names of the Baals from her mouth, and they will be mentioned no more — the elimination of the Baal names from Israel’s liturgical vocabulary is part of the covenant renewal. Personal names compounded with “Baal” were common in the early monarchy (Saul’s son Ish-baal; David’s son Beeliada; 1 Chronicles 8:33; Hosea 14:7) and were later systematically changed in the text to “bosheth” (shame) — e.g., Ish-bosheth, Mephibosheth. The Deuteronomic reform under Josiah systematically destroyed Baal installations and removed the names from the cult; Hosea anticipates this as ultimately a divine act rather than a royal program.
18: In that day I will make a covenant for them with the wild animals, the birds of the sky, and the creatures that crawl on the ground — the new covenant extends to the whole created order, reversing the curse on creation (Genesis 3:14-19; Hosea 9:2). The hostility between humans and animals introduced at the fall is overcome; I will abolish the bow, the sword, and warfare from the land, and make them lie down in safety — the elimination of weapons recalls the eschatological vision of Isaiah 2:4 (beating swords into plowshares) and Micah 4:3. The new covenant inaugurates a creation-wide shalom that the Sinai covenant could not produce because it was constantly violated.
19: I will betroth you to me forever; I will betroth you in righteousness and justice, in covenant loyalty and compassion — the triple betrothal formula is unique in the Hebrew Bible. Righteousness and justice (tsedek umishpat) = the juridical foundations of the relationship; covenant loyalty and compassion (hesed werahamim) = the relational qualities that sustain it. The five terms of Hosea 2:19-20 — tsedek, mishpat, hesed, rahamim, emunah — together describe the full character of YHWH’s covenant commitment. This is the covenant that human failure could not maintain being re-established on the basis of YHWH’s own qualities rather than Israel’s performance.
20: I will betroth you in faithfulness, and you will know the LORD — emunah (faithfulness, steadiness) is the fifth betrothal quality, and the climax is: weyadaʿat ʾet-YHWH, “you will know YHWH.” The daʿat (knowledge) that Hosea identified as the fatal absence in Hosea 4 (“destroyed for lack of knowledge”; “no knowledge of God in the land”) will be the defining feature of the new covenant. The new relationship will be characterized by the very thing the old relationship lacked. Jeremiah 31:34 develops this theme: in the new covenant, everyone will know YHWH from the least to the greatest.
21: In that day I will respond to the heavens, and they will respond to the earth — a cosmic chain of response (ʿanah, to answer/respond): YHWH answers the heavens, the heavens answer the earth, the earth answers the grain/wine/oil, the grain/wine/oil answers Jezreel. This reverses the covenant curse where the heavens “shut up” their rain (Deuteronomy 28:24; 1 Kings 17:1). The chain of divine-natural responsiveness describes a universe working as YHWH designed it, with every level of creation in harmonious response to the one above it, ultimately responsive to YHWH himself.
22: The earth will respond to the grain, the wine, and the oil, and they will respond to Jezreel — the chain of response concludes with Jezreel (“God plants”), the name that carries both judgment (Hosea 1:4-5) and restoration (Hosea 1:11; Hosea 2:22). The name is now fully positive: the cosmic food chain is responsive to the place where God plants his people. The agricultural restoration concretizes the eschatological promise: the same crops that Israel credited to Baal will flow from YHWH’s renewed covenant in the land.
23: I will plant her for myself in the land, and I will show mercy to Lo-ruhamah, and to Lo-ammi I will say ‘You are my people,’ and he will say ‘You are my God’ — the three children’s names are reversed: Israel is planted in the land (Jezreel = God plants), Lo-ruhamah becomes Ruhamah (pitied), Lo-ammi becomes Ammi (my people). The covenant formula “You are my people / You are my God” — the same formula whose dissolution was the most devastating moment of Hosea 1:9 — is fully restored. Paul applies this restoration to Gentile inclusion (Romans 9:25-26); Peter applies it to the church (1 Peter 2:10).
